ARKANSAS FISCAL LEDGER

Interactive Simulator • Report On The Data • GitHub

Power BI Core Corporate Reporting System

Fiscal Procurement Analysis Report

An Assessment of Structural Variance and Vendor Concentration profiles in State Procurement Systems

Summary

This study evaluates the statewide spending habits of Arkansas public sectors for the current fiscal year. By matching master contracts with individual purchase transaction logs, we are able to see exactly where taxpayer funding is utilized and where program costs have shifted over time. The goal of this review is to provide clear insight into state government operations without burying the findings in complex code or technical jargon.

The Macro View of Statewide Spending

When a public sector project is first launched, the state legislature approves a maximum funding limit for the contract. This represents the official baseline budget. However, as projects progress, agencies issue purchase orders to actually allocate and spend that capital. To evaluate how well agencies stick to these plans, our reporting platform uses a metric called budget overage variance. This calculation measures the specific dollar amount that actual ordered funds diverge from the initial maximum caps.

When we look at the entire state ledger, the data reveals a total budget overage variance of negative five billion two hundred thirty seven million nine hundred sixty five thousand two hundred seventy two dollars and fifteen cents. Because this is a negative number, it proves that the state successfully kept active spending far below the maximum allowable funding ceilings. Operating more than five billion dollars under the approved baseline shows a highly conservative approach to releasing purchase orders compared to the absolute limits set by initial contracts.

Agency Deep Dives and Vendor Concentration

While the overall state operates safely under its budget caps, funding remains highly concentrated among a small group of specialized vendors. To understand where the largest pools of money go, we isolated the state departments with the heaviest transaction volumes.

The Department of Human Services commands some of the most massive technology and administrative agreements on the ledger. They rely on external corporations to manage complex data processing and software systems. For example, Gainwell Technologies holds a single data processing contract valued at over four hundred thirty four million dollars. Optum Government Solutions also manages multiple contracts in this space totaling over one hundred seventy million dollars.

Within the Department of Correction, spending is heavily focused on essential logistics like staple groceries and frozen foods to supply the prison system. Two primary vendors dominate this space. Robbins Sales Company holds a contract valued at over two hundred thirty seven million dollars, and National Food Group holds a separate agreement for over one hundred eighty seven million dollars. Centralizing these massive supply lines creates purchasing efficiency but leaves the state heavily reliant on just two food distributors.

We also see unique partnerships between different public entities. The University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences appears constantly across the ledger as a top tier service provider for the Department of Human Services, the Department of Health, and the Department of Veterans Affairs. They provide professional medical services, therapy, and administrative support, acting as a central healthcare safety net for various state operations.

Strategic Recommendations

To maintain this strong fiscal position, the platform utilizes a predictive simulation model. By adjusting target parameters, we can see how modest policy updates yield significant taxpayer protection.

While the current budget overage variance shows a safe five billion dollar buffer, enforcing a strict ceiling on individual vendor overruns ensures that no single contract can unexpectedly spike and erase those savings. Continuing to monitor the spending velocity of top tier accounts like Gainwell Technologies and Robbins Sales Company will balance vital public service delivery with aggressive fiscal accountability.
